structure_blocks.text: You will establish a final price with the contractor based on the audit findings. The goal is to finalize this within 90 days of receiving the audit report. You must also inform CPAG of the settlement terms and how the audit's concerns were addressed.

structure_blocks.text: When you receive monies directly from contractors related to claim by Canada, they must be forwarded to the Finance Branch accompanied by a detailed memorandum as outlined in the Directive on Public Money and Receivables as noted in section 7 an subsection 9(3) of the Financial Administration Act .

structure_blocks.heading: Receipt and deposit of monies
structure_blocks.text: This memorandum should outline the reason for the payment, include the name of the client, the contract number, and contact details of the responsible buyer with a copy to the client and CPAG .

structure_blocks.heading: Receipt and deposit of monies
structure_blocks.text: Outside the national capital region, the destination for these monies is to the appropriate Procurement Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) regional director's office.

raw_text:
Cost reimbursable contracts

In cost reimbursable contracts, contractors are compensated for costs that are both reasonable and properly incurred in the performance of the work.

Upon completion of the work, on all cost reimbursable contracts meeting the cost threshold, you must place on the procurement file a certification to the effect that the final amount paid represents a reasonable price. This certification should be based on the findings of a cost audit, if one was done.

The agreed final price, after all reconciliations, is then formalized through a contract amendment known as the "finalization of cost amendment", which adjusts the total contract price to reflect the final price and specifies that no further claims may be submitted.

The audit provision in contracts exceeding $50,000 with Canadian businesses, allows for the determination of the actual costs incurred to determine the final contract cost of cost reimbursable contracts, and if it is reasonable.

All cost reimbursable contract require a cost submission at the completion of the contract. All multi-year cost reimbursable contracts, except repair and overhaul services, will include a provision for an annual cost submission as a mandatory deliverable. For repair and overhaul service contracts, an annual cost submission is at your discretion and the audit agency’s.

Cost audit

The process for selecting cost reimbursable contracts for audit involves several key aspects described below.

Audit selection

You, as the buyer, will select contracts for a cost audit after consultation with the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG). Selection criteria include all contracts associated with Major Crown projects and if risks of significant overpayment are apparent.

Documentation submission

You must provide CPAG with a copy of the contract documents and a copy of all cost submissions received for those contracts selected for audit.

Audit report

CPAG will forward to you a copy of the audit report along with an audit notification form outlining any overpayments or comments requiring approval.

Final price determination

You will establish a final price with the contractor based on the audit findings. The goal is to finalize this within 90 days of receiving the audit report. You must also inform CPAG of the settlement terms and how the audit's concerns were addressed.

Certification of final price

Once the contractor agrees with the final price, you must certify that the charged price is reasonable and aligns with the contract.

Receipt and deposit of monies

When you receive monies directly from contractors related to claim by Canada, they must be forwarded to the Finance Branch accompanied by a detailed memorandum as outlined in the Directive on Public Money and Receivables as noted in section 7 an subsection 9(3) of the Financial Administration Act .

This memorandum should outline the reason for the payment, include the name of the client, the contract number, and contact details of the responsible buyer with a copy to the client and CPAG .

Outside the national capital region, the destination for these monies is to the appropriate Procurement Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) regional director's office.

The agreed final price, after all reconciliations, is then formalized through a contract amendment known as the "finalization of cost amendment", which adjusts the total contract price to reflect the final price and specifies that no further claims may be submitted.

The audit provision in contracts exceeding $50,000 with Canadian businesses, allows for the determination of the actual costs incurred to determine the final contract cost of cost reimbursable contracts, and if it is reasonable.

All cost reimbursable contract require a cost submission at the completion of the contract. All multi-year cost reimbursable contracts, except repair and overhaul services, will include a provision for an annual cost submission as a mandatory deliverable. For repair and overhaul service contracts, an annual cost submission is at your discretion and the audit agency’s.

The process for selecting cost reimbursable contracts for audit involves several key aspects described below.

You, as the buyer, will select contracts for a cost audit after consultation with the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG). Selection criteria include all contracts associated with Major Crown projects and if risks of significant overpayment are apparent.
